data.title: Games
data.authors: G Catak, S Lieb, F Mazrek, T Thormählen, P Siriaraya, S Adachi, V Visch, R Spijkerman, S Nakajima, G Ataguba, O Oyebode, G Chan, R Orji, S Kriglstein, J Flitcroft, J Marshall, F Leo Tabios, E Varjo, G Wallner
data.description:
The diverse and growing numbers of individuals engaged with digital games give a strong testimony to the appeal that they have for people in today’s society. Recent reports have estimated that there are more than 3.0 billion gamers worldwide, a number which is predicted to grow continuously in the coming years [1]. The demographics of gamers have also become more diverse, with a recent survey estimating that 44 percent of adults aged 50 or more have played digital games at least once per month [2].
This increasing popularity of digital games has led researchers to question whether the techniques which have been used so successfully to motivate people to spend significant proportions of their time engaged with the gaming activities, could similarly be used to encourage people to pursue serious and purposeful activities.
